data.title: AngloGold Ashanti: Good With The Strategy, But Expensive When You Run The Numbers
data.description:
AngloGold Ashanti (AU) is, in my opinion, one of the two competitors for the 'silver medal' in gold mining, alongside Newmont (NEM), and behind the industry leader Agnico (AEM). While NEM is going bigger and wider, AU seems to have focused on being leaner and meaner - and been richly rewarded for it so far. Perhaps a bit too richly. In recent years, AU has moved its primary listing from South Africa to the NYSE and gotten rid of non-Tier-1 assets in Brazil and Colombia while building an entire new Tier-1 district in Nevada. The market loves this kind of high-efficiency, high-margin play, and the stock has delivered stellar results in 2025 - 266% growth and an 8% dividend, including specials, which was significant idiosyncratic alpha over its peers.

However, my main interest here is not in their overall strategy, which I quite like. It is purely on the numbers, based on AU's reported assets - and those numbers suggest to me that the bull case for AU is now highly speculative.

Valuing the Resources and Reserves

AU's gold mineral reserves have gone from 31.2 million ounces in 2024 to 36.5 million ounces in 2025, with strong gains coming from the Sukari acquisition in Africa and from the Pre-Feasibility Study (PFS) at the Arthur Gold Project, which has added the Merlin reserves to its total. The Measured and Indicated Mineral Resources (M&I), which are exclusive of reserves, stand at 68 million ounces. Another 49.3 million ounces is inferred gold resources.

For 2026, AU has given guidance for the All-In Sustaining Costs (AISC) at the group level to be at USD 1990 per ounce at the higher end of the range (see page 3 here). Meanwhile, the average gold price received per ounce for 2025 was USD 3468 per ounce (for Q4, this was at USD 4171). Given the generally bullish consensus on gold, I think USD 3500 per ounce is a reasonable assumption price, giving us a profit of USD 1500 per ounce. Going strictly by the reserves estimate of 36.5 million ounces, then we get a total undiscounted pre-tax cash flow of around USD 55 billion.

Given that 60% of these reserves are in Africa, a blended discount rate of about 8% is reasonable, along with a blended tax/royalty rate of 35%. This gives us an annual cash flow of about USD 2.5 billion, which is discounted over 14 years at 8% to a present NPV of approximately USD 21 billion for the reserves. The current market cap is 45 billion, or 2.15x the proven floor value of the assets. This does not take into account the significant number of measured and indicated resources. Since the current market cap exceeds the NPV of the reserves by about USD 24 billion, that's how much the market is pricing these resources at.

Using the same calculation as for the reserves and applying them to the resources - 1500 USD per ounce margin, 8% discount rate, and 35% royalties and taxes - we get an NPV for the 68 million ounces of resources at about USD 27.5 billion. The standard practice is to discount the M&I by 50%. This gives us an M&I value of 13.75 billion. Thus, the NPV of a floor of proven reserves (USD 21 billion) and risk-adjusted value of M&I resources (USD 13.75 billion) give us a total adjusted asset value of USD 34.75 billion, as opposed to a market cap of USD 45 billion. This is a P/NAV of around 1.3x. For comparison, a Baker Steel report from early 2026 pegs the average P/NAV for major gold miners at around 0.75x (this was before the modest March correction in prices). This is an overvaluation that looks hard to justify even if you are speculatively betting on certain factors working out very well (see the Risks section below).

Upward Divergence From Peers

The above indicates that there should have been some point of divergence between the P/NAV of AU and of other major miners such as Agnico and Newmont. Indeed, if you look at the 5-year comparison (see above), until 2023, AU traded at a discount to AEM and NEM. It then closed that discount as it moved its primary listing to the NYSE in 2023 and then started diverging upwards on fundamentals around 2025 as it acquired Augusta Gold, cleared its net debt in December, and started generating excitement around the Arthur project. There was also likely some re-rating from a constantly improving risk profile, as its newer assets are largely in Tier-1 jurisdictions.

You can see from the 1-year comparison (see below) AU has shot up 140% to AEM's 78% and NEM's 111%. The reasons for this rise are very valid, and by all accounts, AU looks well positioned to capitalise on the Arthur project. However, the numbers I have run in the previous section are all based on reports released very recently (26th March 2026), and I think they strongly suggest that the stock has priced in a lot of good news up front, rather than waiting for delivery. This narrows the scope for growth in the stock in the medium term and leaves it dependent on asset price as the main growth driver from this point on.

My opinion is that AU has had about as many upward re-ratings as it can digest for the medium term, and the larger part of the 3-5 year stock growth might have already played out.

Risks

The neutral case that I have laid out for AU is the view that arises based on conservative assumptions, which is usually how you treat a large stock. But AU having solid assets, there is always a possibility that if enough variables turn out to be better than expected, the stock might outperform the conservative view by some distance.

Perhaps most importantly, the geology of the Beatty District in Nevada (where the Arthur project is located) seems to be quite consistent. These are high-grade surface oxides, which typically throw up few problems - Barrick/Newmont's neighbouring JV has had a historical conversion rate of around 75%, the same as Round Mountain, which is also in Nevada. That would bring down the current P/NAV from 1.3x to around 1.05x, which is a lot more reasonable. Also worth noting here is that even though the average P/NAV for senior miners (quoted by Baker Steel above) is around 0.75x, industry leader Agnico often trades above 1.4x. If you were to assign the same kind of premium to AU as Agnico, then 1.05x doesn't look that bad. In my opinion, considering that roughly 50% of its current assets are still in Africa, this might be a bit too much, but a case can still be made that 1.1x-1.2x is a reasonable band for a premium miner like AU, which is actively shifting towards Tier-1 jurisdictions.

The Arthur project also has high margins, with the PFS reporting AISC at USD 954 per ounce. So, if you're very bullish on gold and are sold on the USD 5,500-6,000 year-end targets for the metal, then you could also see the Nevada district contribute disproportionately to AU's growth story (though rising asset prices would mostly have a secular effect across the sector). What also looks good for AU is its strong cash position in 2025 (USD 897 million net positive) that suggests that it can fund the entire USD 3.6 billion capex for the Arthur project on its own. In short, the Arthur project is undoubtedly a fantastic asset, and even though a lot of it has been priced in already, someone who's willing to go all out betting on it could find worse bets to take. It's just that you don't usually come to the blue-chip players for these kinds of bets, for a number of reasons, so those are not assumptions that you naturally make when analysing a stock like this.

Conclusion

I think what we are seeing with AngloGold Ashanti is the first major gold miner to truly see a growth premium attached to it on the basis of the new asset price levels for gold - probably because they are the first major miners to declare new resources (the Arthur project and others in Nevada) post the massive run-up in asset prices, they have seen proportionately a greater premium being given to their new discoveries. There exists a decent buy case if you are willing to speculate on the geological consistency of Nevada's gold mines, which would be justified. Going by the present reported numbers, however, AU stock is undoubtedly expensive compared to its peers.

For now, any sell case for AU is purely conjectural - maybe NEM starts delivering great numbers post its "trough" year, or maybe AEM announces concrete and attractive plans for its next phase of growth (something it needs to do eventually). But until any of that happens, AU remains a solid stock worth holding on to for the long term - just one that is too expensive and has too little headroom for medium-term growth to get into at the current price levels. If a renewed bull run in gold sends it back to USD 110-120 levels, it might be worth looking into cycling out of this stock at that point, but for now, hold on until and unless another high-quality play in gold reveals itself definitively. Add if you want to play it like a spec stock, but then always remember to distinguish the blue-chip nature of the company from the speculative nature of your play.

data.title: Zhihu: Has Growth Potential Even Though The AI Fallout Continues
data.description:
Zhihu Inc. (ZH), a provider of online content in China, got off to a great start in 2026 with a powerful rally, but ZH seems to have become a casualty of turmoil in the stock market due to various reasons. For instance, AI stocks, especially those on the software side, have fallen greatly, which found its way back to ZH, something the release of the Q4 FY2025 report on March 25 was not able to stop. However, there are still a number of reasons to be long on ZH, which include the new possibilities that are opening up due to advances in AI technology.

A great start to 2026 turns upside down

My previous article from December 2025 offered a number of reasons why it could be a good time to get in on ZH. For instance, the stock had regained its footing after a large reduction in the stock price by going sideways, which could portend a move higher. ZH was also close to a price level where it had a prior history of reversing course. Accordingly, I decided to rate ZH a buy in the article.

The chart above showed why it initially appeared the article made a great call in December because ZH went on a huge rally not long thereafter, which resulted in a high of $4.52 per ADS in January for a YTD gain of 37.8%, but the decline in the stock that followed wiped out all gains and then some. ZH is now down 20.4% YTD after closing at $2.61 per ADS on March 27. ZH experienced a drastic change in fortunes in just a couple of months.

What is weighing on ZH?

The change in fortunes stands out. Such a major change is unlikely to have happened for no particular reason. It's more likely the result of a confluence of multiple factors conspiring to take down the stock. For starters, it is worth mentioning that there has been an ongoing selloff in AI-related stocks, particularly those on the software side.

Many high-profile names in the space have been going down since January, and this seems to have rubbed off on ZH. For example, Palantir (PLTR) and AppLovin (APP) got off to a good start in early January, only to steadily lose ground in the months that followed. The same can be said of many other software stocks tied to AI.

ZH is primarily a provider of online content, and AI is not a major contributor financially, but ZH has increasingly looked at AI as a potential driver of growth, especially since the content on the platform can be, for instance, used to train large language models. If AI stumbles, then that could affect the outlook for ZH, as well as affect how investors perceive a stock like ZH.

Adding to the struggles for the stock more recently is the war in Iran and the restrictions imposed in the Strait of Hormuz, which has led to a general selloff in stocks, something ZH has not been able to escape. On the other hand, it is worth mentioning that while the stock has sold off, this also means the stock is now oversold with an RSI reading in the 20s. This suggests a bounce in the stock could be near, if only because ZH has fallen by so much in recent weeks.

Long ZH is worth considering according to the balance sheet

The earlier chart shows how the release of the most recent, or Q4 FY2025 report did not provide a lift, but it is still worth noting how the report did not leave the bulls empty-handed. For starters, the latest updates to the balance sheet show that after the recent conclusion of FY2025, cash and cash equivalents stood at $481.78M. What’s so significant about the number is that this is twice what ZH is valued at with the stock at $2.61 per ADS and a current market cap of $232M.

This by itself is one argument in favor of long ZH, but the argument becomes stronger if one considers that current assets include another $120.25M in short-term investments and $4.29M in term deposits. Non-current assets on the balance sheet include $22.66M in long-term investments and $30.03M in term deposits. In contrast, total debt stands at just $10.3M.

If all the assets and liabilities are added up, then the balance sheet points to a book value of $6.93 per ADS. This means that ZH is available at well below book value with a price-to-book of 0.38x and the stock at $2.61 per ADS. While ZH looks undervalued based on the balance sheet, that really depends on the income statement, since what happens in the latter can add to but also subtract from the former.

The income statement came in mixed

While the bulls had some things to take solace in, the latest income statement also showed some less than desirable developments. These developments detract from the balance sheet, no matter how strong the latter may be. For instance, ZH hit a milestone by achieving its first full-year of non-GAAP profitability in the recently concluded FY2025.

ZH ended FY2025 with non-GAAP net income of RMB37.87M, or $5.42M, up from a net loss of RMB96.27M in FY2024. In terms of GAAP, which includes a number of items like share-based compensation not included in non-GAAP, ZH was still in the red with a net loss attributable to shareholders of RMB192.9M, or $27.58M.

It also represents an increase compared to the loss of RMB171.80M in FY2024, but there would have been a decrease if ZH did not incur a goodwill impairment to the tune of RMB126.34M in FY2025. Per share, ZH posted a GAAP loss of RMB2.41, or $0.34 per ADS, in FY2025, more than the loss of RMB1.88 in FY2024. Loss from operations was RMB507.29M, or $72.54M, in FY2025, more than the loss of RMB481.08M in FY2024, but this is with the previously mentioned goodwill impairment of RMB126.34M.

However, while some things got better, there was more that got worse. Revenue shrank to RMB2,749.0M, or $393.1M, in FY2025, less than RMB3,598.91M in FY2024. Gross margin of 59.9% in FY2025 was 70 basis points lower than in FY2024. Moreover, ZH ended FY2025 on a soft note as the numbers in the final quarter of the fiscal year got significantly worse.

Revenue decreased from RMB859.2M in Q4 FY2024 to RMB643.5M, or $92.0M, in Q4 FY2025, which resulted in a GAAP net loss attributable to shareholders of RMB208.63M, or $29.84M, worse than the net profit of RMB86.27M a year ago. Yes, this does include the previously mentioned goodwill impairment, but even in terms of non-GAAP, which leaves out the impairment, net loss of RMB39.31M, or $5.64M, in Q4 FY2025 was worse than the net profit of RMB97.07M in Q4 FY2024.

How can ZH invigorate growth?

The changes on the income statement detract from what would otherwise be a very solid balance sheet. If the income statement stays in the red, then that will eat up the cash, no matter how much of it there is. If ZH is to make itself more appealing to potential buyers, it will need to improve the income statement. Specifically, ZH needs to grow, the top line in particular, since that will open up venues when it comes to the bottom line.

The good news here is that there are possibilities for ZH when it comes to growth. ZH is essentially an online content provider, text in particular. This includes questions and answers like in Quora, which is probably what ZH is best known for, but ZH also provides many other forms of text content, content that could be used to drive growth, especially when combined with new possibilities provided by AI.

For instance, ZH has increasingly become a platform for fictional stories. The thing is that advances in AI technology have opened up new possibilities for all this original text content. For example, thanks to ever-improving text-to-video tools, stories could be turned into full-fledged videos.

The success of, for example, the app ReelShort shows people are willing to pay for microfilms or short videos lasting about 90 seconds on drama or other types of genres. These popular videos are made the traditional way with live actors and so on, but this is no longer necessary with AI.

The issue here for ZH is that with the large library of text content it already has at its disposal, and with advanced text-to-video tools like the recently released Seedance 2.0 from ByteDance, which has been making waves for its ability to create high-quality, realistic-looking, AI-generated videos, ZH could turn itself into a provider of video entertainment at relatively low cost. These can then be accessed through subscriptions. Something that approaches, but not quite what a Netflix (NFLX) does, for example.

Keep in mind that while others also have access to text-to-video tools, they don’t necessarily have the original text needed to generate videos, which means they have to resort to copying existing ideas, stories, characters, scripts, and so on. This will become problematic since movie studios and other relevant stakeholders can be expected to bring to bear the full weight of the law to protect their interests.

ZH, on the other hand, has its own stories or original content to work with and does not have to violate anyone's intellectual property. ZH might be able to take advantage of the advances of AI in a way that most cannot because they would need to first acquire original content, something that could be costly in terms of finances and time. In other words, ZH might actually have much more growth potential than it has shown lately with recent reports.

Investor takeaways

ZH put in a mixed report with its final report for FY2025. ZH managed to achieve its first full-year non-GAAP net income, and it also managed to significantly reduce net losses in terms of GAAP, something that would work well in combination with a strong balance sheet that features cash and cash equivalents far in excess of what ZH is worth in terms of market cap.

However, the progress was offset by the soft end to the fiscal year, with the company regressing in Q4 FY2025. Revenue also continues to shrink, which will increasingly become problematic if it continues. This negated whatever pop the stock might have gotten otherwise from the progress made. ZH has also been negatively affected by the slide in many AI stocks since the early part of 2026 and, more recently, by troubles in the Middle East for the stock market.

Yet it needs pointing out that while ZH has embraced the use of AI, there are important differences between ZH and the affected AI software stocks. Unlike many among the latter, ZH does not trade at lofty valuations due to AI. ZH is instead valued at half the net cash on the books and by even less if other assets on the books are converted to cash. Unlike many other stocks, ZH did not see a huge run-up in its stock price due to AI, and any further slide in the stock price will lower already low valuations to even lower valuations.

While ZH has yet to show it can avoid net losses consistently, the cash hoard provides it a level of protection. The cash buffer provides ZH with time to work on getting the bottom line to the point that losses are no longer something to be worried about. FY2025 was thus a milestone, although ZH will need to build on it.

ZH did end FY2025 on a soft note, but Q4 FY2025 is only one quarter, and it remains to be seen if upcoming quarters will resemble the final quarter or FY2025 as a whole, which did show notable progress, especially in terms of profitability. In addition, there is reason to be optimistic that ZH can drive growth higher because it retains a lot of potential it has yet to tap into.

There is a lot of original content at ZH, something that is likely to become more valuable as increasingly capable AI-powered tools like text-to-video from companies like ByteDance open up new opportunities for monetization that were not there before. Combine this untapped growth potential with low valuations, which includes being available at much less than net cash on the books, and long ZH is worth considering.

ZH deserves the benefit of the doubt that it has what it takes to ignite growth, and I am thus bullish on ZH at current valuations. It is possible the stock heads lower in today’s shaky stock environment with global tensions running high, but at half the net cash, or even less, and with increasing opportunities to turn text content into sources of income, long ZH is warranted.

data.title: Weekly Market Pulse: War - What Is It Good For?
data.description:
Sentiment about the economy is negative right now, but that isn’t a new development. The University of Michigan consumer sentiment survey is near its all-time worst reading, which was set just a few years ago in 2022. The expectations portion of that survey is currently at levels only seen in the mid-70s near the bottom of the 73/74 bear market, the double-dip recession of 80/81, the first Gulf War in 1990, the 2008 financial crisis, the 2011 Euro crisis, and the inflation surge and bear market of 2022. Consumer sentiment is not just sour but on par with some of the worst economic times of my life. Consumer Confidence (from the Conference Board) improved slightly in February but has been in a downtrend since peaking in 2018. Our sour mood isn’t new, and it isn’t specifically about anything going on right now.

The U of Michigan survey paints a dire picture that is hard to square with the actual data. This economy has its share of problems, but this is a long way from the circumstances of 1975, 1980, or 2008. When I look at the current economic data, there is a mix of good and bad. And for most of the bad readings, it is a matter of rate of change, not current level.

Negative Economic Trends

  • Real disposable personal income is up 1.8% over the last year. The average since 1990 is about 2.6%
  • Real retail sales are up just 0.7% year over year, below the average since 1993 of 2.1%. On the other hand, real retail sales spiked during the COVID spending spree and have essentially flatlined since 2021
  • Light vehicle sales are down 1.4%, and heavy truck sales are down 17.4% over the last year. The latter is a pretty good recession indicator
  • Inflation is still running higher than the Fed’s target, with core CPI up 2.5% and core PCE up 3.1% over the last year
  • Existing and new single-family home sales are down 1.4% and 11.3%, respectively
  • Job openings are down 6.5% in the last year
  • Labor force participation is down to 62% from 62.5% a year ago

Positive Economic Trends

  • Industrial production is up 1.2% over the last 3 months
  • The savings rate is up to 4.5% from 4% 3 months ago (I know this one could be interpreted negatively)
  • Most of the business surveys (PMIs and regional Fed surveys) are positive. Philly Fed (18.1), ISM Services (56.1), ISM Manufacturing (52.4), KC Fed (11.0) have all improved recently
  • Real personal consumption expenditures are up 2.4% versus the long-term average of 2.2%
  • Inventories/sales ratios are down for total business, wholesale, and retail
  • Durable goods orders are up 12.1% over the last year (9.6% ex-transportation)
  • Non-defense capital goods ex-aircraft (core capital goods) orders are up 5.4% yoy
  • Initial jobless claims continue to be very low at 210k

I could also point to less well-known indicators like:

  • Redbook year-over-year change in same-store sales up 6.7% last week
  • OpenTable change in seated diners is up 8% in the US and 10% globally
  • Income tax withholding continues to rise
  • The Fed’s weekly economic index is 2.86, well above the 0 line that marks recession
  • The 3-month average of the CFNAI is -0.01, with a reading of 0.0 meaning trend growth; it has actually recently improved some
  • Q1 estimate for GDPNow is at 2%, right on trend (but it is early)
  • CARTS (Chicago Advance Retail Trade Summary) is running around 4%
  • New business formations are near an all-time high

It is important to remember that all parts of the economy don’t grow in sync; there are always parts of the economy performing better and parts performing worse than the economy as a whole. It isn’t unusual to have mixed data, and while the negatives and positives may have changed, I think what we see above is an economy that’s pretty typical of the last few years. That may seem hard to believe, with all that has happened, but it is accurate. It is confirmed by the stability we see in interest rates.

The 10-year Treasury yield has risen some recently, but at 4.44% is just a tad over the average of the last 3 years and well within the range of that period. The nominal 10-year Treasury yield is a proxy for the year-over-year change in NGDP (real growth + inflation) and in this case is likely reflecting a small change in short-term inflation expectations. That is, obviously, a function of the Iran War, but the change is not out of the ordinary for what we’ve experienced over the last 3 years.

The 2-year Treasury yield has also risen recently and is now trading higher than Fed Funds (but less than the 3-year average), an indication that rate cuts have been priced out of the market. Given current conditions that include an inflation rate still well above the Fed’s target, I’m not sure that’s a bad thing.

Real rates (10-year TIPS yield) are also up a little recently and a little above the 3-year average. The rise so far is small, and so I would hesitate to assign a cause (never reason from a price change). A negative view would be a developing distaste for US debt, while a positive view would be better real growth expectations. In either case the change is too small to mean all that much. Maybe more interesting is that over the last two weeks, the change in the 10-year yields (real and nominal) has produced a fall in the 10-year breakeven inflation rate. The 5-year, 5-year forward inflation expectation rate has fallen too. (the 5-year, 5-year is a measure of inflation expectations over the 5-year period that starts 5 years from now). Whatever inflation angst the Iran War is producing isn’t expected to last.

Long-term inflation expectations have also been steady. We’ve seen a rise in very short-term (1 year) inflation expectations, but even there the change is less than 1%.

Obviously, the Iran War is negative for the global economy in the short term, but the reaction in markets so far has been incredibly tame. That could change quickly, so maybe investors are too sanguine, but the odds on sanity breaking out aren’t zero.

Sentiment about markets is not as negative as the public’s attitude about the economy. Sentiment is negative but not at an extreme yet and may not reach an extreme if the war is resolved fairly soon. The American Association of Individual Investor sentiment poll shows bears at 49.8%, bulls at 32.1%, and neutral at 18.1%. So yes, there are more bears than bulls, but major bottoms require more extreme readings. If this is a bear market – which I don’t think it is – I’d expect the bull cohort to fall at least to the low-20s and probably into the teens.

The NAAIM exposure index is at 68.52, but a market low usually takes that down to less than 50. The equity only put/call ratio hit 0.92 last week, but it usually takes a reading over 1 to call a bottom. The overall put/call ratio hit 1.12 last week but usually needs to get over 1.2 for a bottom.

The percent of stocks above their 200-day moving average has fallen to 44%, which is getting closer, but it usually falls into the 30s in a correction. The percent above their 50-day moving average is down to 20.4, but corrections usually take it into the teens and often into single digits.

The stock market reaction to the Iran War is actually, I think, quite a bit milder than even the headlines. This correction has been mostly about growth stocks and, to be even more specific, technology stocks. The Russell 1000 Growth Index ETF peaked on October 29th last year, and since then is down nearly 16%. During that same time frame, the Russell 1000 Value Index ETF is up 3.4%.

If growth stocks started their correction over 3 months before the start of the war, how much impact has the war really had? If the sector being affected most is technology, can we really assign the blame to the Iran War? The war is certainly dramatic and constantly in the headlines, but, at least so far, it isn’t really having as big an impact on the markets as is widely perceived. Anyone who’s been doing this for more than a few years knew that growth stocks – and, again, technology stocks, to be specific – would come back to earth. That doesn’t mean I – or anyone else – knew when that would happen, but what I find interesting is that it’s been going on since late October, and it seems like people only recently noticed and decided the war must be driving stocks lower. Maybe. Or maybe the war is just a good excuse to take some profits.

The companies that are building our AI future are good companies, but their stock prices were starting to price in a future that was built more on hype than reality. I’ve said all along that this transition, like all technology transitions before it, will take longer than the optimists expect. A very large fraction of US GDP is produced by companies that not only aren’t in the S&P 500 but aren’t even public. Nearly half of US GDP and employment can be attributed to small businesses (less than 500 employees). How long do you think it will take these companies to get up to speed on AI? Most of them don’t have staff who can dedicate a significant portion of their time to figuring out how to use AI and how to apply it to their business. I don’t know how long it will take to feel the impact of AI on the economy as a whole, but I’d bet it is a lot longer than the year or two all the AI hypesters are talking about.

Meanwhile, earnings estimates continue to rise, and profit margins for the S&P 500 companies are at all-time highs. Earnings for Q1 are now expected to rise by 13%, the sixth consecutive quarter of double-digit, year-over-year growth. Companies don’t seem to think the war will impact their business much; a higher than average percent of companies are providing positive guidance on future earnings, while the reverse is true for negative guidance.

The S&P 500 is still not particularly cheap, but it is quite a bit less expensive than it was a few months ago, partly due to great earnings growth and partly due to stock prices coming down. The S&P 500 currently trades for 19-20 times forward earnings, which is still more than the 10-year average (about 18) and a lot more than the long-term average (closer to 15). But that doesn’t mean there aren’t good companies to buy today at reasonable prices; I certainly find a lot more of them today than I did just a few months ago.

I don’t know what will happen with the war, and neither does anyone else, but it does seem that a negative outcome – there are plenty to choose from – is becoming the consensus. Some who were selling stocks last week were no doubt selling because they fear a very negative outcome from the war. In fact, it may have been a majority. But war is one of man’s most unpredictable endeavors, and there are a range of potential outcomes, some negative, some positive. And it could easily be some of both.

It is times like this when diversification proves its worth. Growth stocks (IWF) are down nearly 13% YTD, but value stocks (FNDX) are flat to up a bit. Even after a correction, international value stocks (FNDF) are up nearly 5% on the year, and the major international index (EFA) is only down about 2%. Commodities (COMT) are up 36%, and gold (IAU) is up 4.7%. REITs (VNQ) are down less than 1% even in a rising rate environment. Short-term TIPS (STIP) are up YTD, while intermediate-term Treasuries (VGIT) are down just a fraction of a percent. Small-cap value stocks (VBR) and mid-cap stocks (IJH) are up YTD. * There have been plenty of places to hide.

I don’t want to minimize the impact of the Iran War. War is always awful and to be avoided if at all possible. But the impact of the war on markets, so far, has been fairly mild and has served mainly to correct overpriced technology stocks. That would have happened at some point anyway, in my opinion; the war was just a catalyst. But if you’re feeling some angst about things and your portfolio is taking a hit you didn’t expect, you might need to think about your risk tolerance and the asset allocation that fits your situation.

data.title: Goldman Sachs BDC's Risky Math: Buying The Discount, Ignoring The Drift
data.description:
When analyzing the Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) in the context of the current market and macro conditions, several conflicting factors come into play. The pressures over the past few months have been amplified by the Iran war. However, signs of fatigue and unwinding in AI trades (markets uncomfortable with long-duration assumptions and high valuations) were already starting to show up since October last year. In such markets, BDCs do not function as defensives either because of leveraged credit positions by definition. So, the macro and market environment is not unusually supportive for GBDC. Additionally, we are already talking about a stickier-than-expected inflation regime ahead with slow growth, and stagflation risks are probably one of the biggest deterrents for BDCs with growing credit risks and little income growth. The rates scenario also potentially presents the worst of uncertainties, with credit stress concerns far from over (if rates remain higher for longer) and the declining overall rates cycle pressuring income for longer-term horizon investors.

GSBD is a high-quality and high-yield credit instrument, no doubt, and has been correcting in the recent past, reflecting some of the macro worries. The Q4 2025 results and commentary also show a mix of green shoots (like improvements in non-accruals) and underlying tensions that should be watched (like an increase in watchlist loans and reliance on non-cash).

Overall, the fundamentals and macro/market conditions do not look clean. However, this analysis looks into why the GSBD stock still becomes a buy, despite the not-so-perfect positioning. The core support comes from the ~30% discount it is trading at today. That, coupled with the fact that most of the fundamental stress ahead (in a realistic but aggressive stress scenario) appears to be cushioned by 2-3 years of even a stressed dividend (that’s actually strong for a credit asset), makes GSBD a buy on the overall valuation and income math.

Compressing Income - Return Math Intact

Let's start with the numbers that show what is actually happening already. Since the end of 2024, investment income has shrunk significantly - NII per share has fallen by over 20%, from ~$0.48 to ~$0.37. According to the management commentary in the Q4 2025 earnings call, yield on book has compressed from ~10.3% to ~9.9% during this period, which can be attributed to the rates normalization regime we are in (until stagflation risks started emerging recently). Along with a period of lower yields, expenses have not come down commensurately - they remain sticky around the ~$43m mark, which means the drop in yields and income flows through almost directly to the net income.

Dividends have also been cut (even after including the effects of a special dividend since 2025), as seen in the chart below. And all this even before any real credit stress has started to emerge.

GSBD's forward outlook also looks messy in the face of macro uncertainties. If rates continue to decline, income could be further pressured. On the other hand, if stagflation effects start showing up, credit stress becomes more relevant. Additionally, part of the current income (~9%) includes Payment in Kind (PIK), which is non-cash and could be linked to stressed or modified loans that could challenge durability. The overall environment appears to be one where GSBD is set up to best manage the portfolio rather than being in a phase where income expands and grows.

To account for a reasonably aggressive stress scenario and check whether GSBD's discount and income setup accounts for it, I have assumed that dividends settle somewhere meaningfully lower than the peak ~$0.53 per share seen earlier in 2025. Even if I assume stressors emerge (likely), and NII per share shrinks from ~$0.37 now to ~$0.25 (that is half of Q4 2024 levels), and if I assume that the NAV also drops by 30% (already at ~$12.64, down from ~$13.41 since Q4 2024) to ~$9, the recovery timelines for fresh investments today look more than good for a credit asset.

At say ~$0.25 quarterly dividends (NII almost fully paid out), the ~$3.5 drop in price at that point takes around 3 years to break even. That is as good as a cushion as you will get in the credit world for a scenario that is decently aggressive (assuming rates, credit, and income quality all start moving against the position at once for a strong portfolio with ~93% first lien exposure). I have even maintained the current discount setup in the scenario I discussed (in fact, slightly higher discount at ~32%), with a ~$3.5 drop in NAV corresponding to a similar drop in share prices.

This scenario is not an immediate possibility either, which reduces the ask of the return math calculations I have assumed. As of now, the ~$0.37 NII more than covers the ~$0.32 dividend obligations. Plus, GSBD reports a spillover of ~$0.97 per share to cover temporary setbacks and even keep yields at manageable levels for several quarters.

Mixed Credit Positioning

The credit picture can be read from both sides (supportive factors versus concerns) - signals are mixed. On the surface, things look better with non-accruals down to ~1.9%, from a peak of ~3.4% (and ~2% during the year). But there are signs indicating caution ahead. Watchlist loans have moved from 3.7% to 6% (defined as loans where risks have increased materially since origination, but payments are not more than 120 days past due). The majority of the book still sits in the stable bucket (no material change in risk since origination) but is down sequentially (~91.7%, down from 93%).

Overall, we are not yet talking about widespread credit stress as a caution -, especially looking at the non-accruals data improvement. But the jump in watchlist loans does warrant more monitoring. Especially, if the newer originations are contributing to the watchlist, while the older assets are performing well - that could be a worrying trend investors will need to keep an eye on.

Structurally, one of the supportive factors for GSBD's portfolio is the strong first lien exposure. Also, management constantly and proactively manages exposure, so risk management and underwriting discipline appear strong. For instance, management has reduced exposure to risky ARR loans from ~39% to ~11% through selective strategic exits and new underwriting filters (focus on EBITDA-based investments). GSBD also discussed how it has exited a performing loan at ~0.99 due to AI risk in the earnings call, emphasizing proactive AI risk management even if the numbers do not flag quantitatively. That matters in today's environment of uncertainty, where AI commoditizing several SaaS functionalities is the talk of the town. And software still is one of the largest sector exposures for GSBD (~17.6%). Overall growth prospects of portfolio companies are important to monitor (not only software) - if growth slows, there is not a lot of cushion as borrowers are fairly leveraged too (~5.9x leverage and ~2.0x interest coverage).

All in all, while not extremely concerning, I do not see the credit situation as a clean Buy signal - something that the non-accruals data suggests. A meaningful part of the portfolio (~43%) is still working through older vintages, adding uncertainty in the evolved macro situation new loans are originating from. While a lot of these transitions and macro effects are on my watchlist, there is a lot of structural strength in GSBD's proactive and qualitative risk management approach.

Valuation and Return Math Create Opportunity

Before concluding the thesis, let us look at the net debt-to-equity leverage, which has broadly trended up from ~1.17x in Q4 2024 to ~1.27x now. While not alarming, it shows that flexibility in the balance sheet is reducing, coinciding with a regime of slowing income. This doesn’t break the thesis, but it shows that the margin for error is narrowing.

Overall, as I mentioned at the start, fundamentals are not why I say Buy GSBD - it's the valuation and return math (buying the stock versus buying the business) that clinch the deal for fresh investments. What looks weak (when looking at the declining income, NAV, and a rising leverage) is more than compensated by a ~30% discount to NAV and a supportive yield under stress scenarios too. I keep credit also as a neutral factor at the moment, but should be monitored as signals are mixed.

GSBD is not a stable and fundamentally strong compounder at the moment, and for the current market and macro setup, it is a discounted credit vehicle where yield compensates for instability.

data.title: Nabors Industries: The War Is Only Bullish Once It Ends
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nabors Industries (NBR) has approximately doubled since my prior article:

This probably makes it a good time to revisit the investment thesis, especially in view of the Iran war that is already extending beyond one month, with profound consequences for the global energy complex.

Energy stocks (XLE) have gone up in this time, though not nearly as much as oil prices (CL1:COM):

First-order thinking may suggest the war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz are bullish for Nabors too. However, the situation is more nuanced, and I believe Nabors has more to gain from the cessation of hostilities than their continuation.

The longer the war goes on, the greater the risk of impacted energy infrastructure and suspended drilling operations. As Nabors has bet big on the SANAD joint venture with Saudi Aramco and also has a presence in neighboring Gulf countries, this means a potentially significant EPS hit. Payments from customers may also be at risk.

Conversely, the war's end will bring along demand for rebuilding the damage caused to the oil infrastructure, and that will likely result in Nabors' revenue from the region exceeding any pre-war expectations.

Ultimately, I don't have a geopolitical crystal ball and cannot predict when or how the war will end. That is why I have adjusted my Nabors stance to neutral. My preference currently is for oilfield services stocks that may do well under both the war ends / war continues scenarios.

Recap of the Recent Bullish Factors

In my view, there are several reasons why Nabors' stock started moving up. First, the Quail divestment I have discussed previously allowed meaningful deleveraging - net debt is now below 2.0x EBITDA for the first time in a long while:

Yields on Nabors' traded debt have fallen meaningfully:

Assuaging the debt concern has gone a long way in helping the equity "re-rate."

Second, Saudi Aramco - before the war - had started recalling drilling rigs that were suspended after the change in strategy in early 2024.

Third, U.S. oil rigs also appear to have bottomed, even if so far it has been only an L-shaped bottom, while U.S. gas rigs have been on the rise:

Fourth, and perhaps somewhat a bit irrationally, Nabors and other oilfield services stocks moved up a lot on the U.S. seizure of Venezuela's Maduro. While Venezuela's production seems to have gone up a notch, most observers remain skeptical about it reaching 3 million barrels per day any time soon.

The Gulf Hostilities Are a Double-Edged Knife

This brings us to the "elephant in the room", the war in the Gulf and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that, by some estimates such as Rystad, has taken offline 12 million barrels per day of liquid supply. What is worse, these barrels aren't just accumulating in storage and waiting for the Strait to reopen but have effectively forced production shut-ins.

The market seems to put a premium on oil production - and by implication, oilfield services assets - that are located outside the region but isn't as bullish on assets that are trapped in the Gulf. Even though Nabors has gained over the last month, it is trailing Patterson-UTI (PTEN), a U.S.-focused drilling and completions company, by about 20 points, completely reversing the previous dynamic:

While Nabors would also benefit from its U.S. rigs, it has a much heavier presence in the Gulf region:

Besides the 60+ Saudi rigs, of which only 50% of EBITDA trickles down to Nabors equity holders given the JV, Nabors also runs rigs in Kuwait and Bahrain, two countries that have effectively shut in their entire production. Apart from the non-zero direct kinetic risk for the drilling operations, the bigger issue is that no one in their right mind would pay to drill new wells while they can't market existing production. The affected Gulf governments may even struggle with their fiscal budgets given they can't sell oil, so new drilling won't be their top priority.

On the Saudi side, there have already been significant war-related rig suspensions, but so far these appear to have been offshore only. Arabian Drilling, essentially a competitor to Nabors' SANAD, reported a few days ago that its Saudi land fleet of 39 rigs continues to operate at full capacity for now.

Nonetheless, Saudi Arabia also remains offtake constrained, not production constrained. According to Bloomberg, the Saudi East-West pipeline has now reached its full throughput capacity of 7 million barrels per day, but the limiting factor appears to be the Yanbu loading terminal capacity, which sits much lower at about 4.5 million barrels. In other words, there is zero incentive for Saudi Aramco to recall more rigs until the hostilities end and the Strait reopens.

My feeling is that the more this situation goes on, the more Nabors has to lose. Eventually there will be some supply response in the U.S., and that will lift utilizations and dayrates on the U.S. side, but Nabors' strategy has been "Saudi first" for a number of years now, and that is where the heavy capex has gone, which may have otherwise repaid debt.

In this sense, Nabors is a "Strait open" play right now, like most of the global economy. If the war continues, I expect Nabors, along with other Gulf-heavy oilfield services stocks, to stall or even sell off.

Additional Risks

Oil prices are extremely volatile right now, with the market doing 180-degree turns on any tweet from the administration or media report citing what some anonymous official thinks about the war situation. This magnifies the risk for Nabors too.

Second, if the broader market crashes, Nabors will likely also be affected.

Takeaway

I have been fundamentally bullish on Nabors for a long time now, but geopolitical risk had not been factored into my thesis. Right now, the geopolitical factor is so overwhelming that Nabors is becoming a bit of a binary Hormuz open/Hormuz closed trade.

As I can't predict the geopolitical outcomes, I prefer to sit on the sidelines. I am more interested right now in Nabors's competitor Patterson-UTI, which is a U.S.-focused play. I believe even after the Strait reopens, U.S. drilling activity will see some boost as the oil price floor will likely resettle higher when all this (hopefully) resolves.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lumexa Imaging: Clearer Growth Path Ahead
data.description:
Investment Overview

I wrote about Lumexa Imaging (LMRI) previously with a buy rating, as this is a business that has strong demand tailwinds, supported by demographic trends and a strong scale advantage. I remain bullish on LMRI, as the Q4 2025 quarter gave more data points to make the growth path clearer. Advanced imaging is doing well, de novo is ramping on time, and the balance sheet has deleveraged nicely.

4Q25 Earnings

LMRI reported a solid Q4 2025 result. Total revenue was up ~8% y/y to $267.7 million, with system-wide revenue up ~11%. Net patient service revenue saw $210.5 million, up from $189.8 million. Volume also improved. Consolidated same-center advanced volume grew 12.7%, while system-wide same-center advanced volume grew 9.2%. Looking at the segments, professional fee revenue was up ~11% y/y; management fee and other revenue was almost flat. In terms of profit, adj. EBITDA grew much faster than the topline, at ~19% y/y to $63.8 million, with adj. EBITDA margin up 150 bps y/y to 23.8%. LMRI is still generating a GAAP net loss mostly because of the costs tied to debt refinancing and other transaction costs in its IPO year.

Advanced Imaging

Advanced imaging is clearly doing well, and you can see that in the mix. Advanced procedures are now 30.4% of total procedures, up from 29.3% a year ago. While that alone matters a lot, I also point out that LMRI is not just growing volume; this is the segment where LMRI gets better revenue per scan and margin. In other words, the unit economics are just much better. For context, advanced procedures reimburse ~3x to 4x higher than routine procedures, and management also made clear this was not driven just by one type of scan. An encouraging datapoint given here is that Positron Emission Tomography [PET] volume grew in the mid-teens in 2025 (faster than system-wide same-center advanced volume growth), and management said they have plans to expand PET capacity into more geographies (which should drive more growth).

The question is whether LMRI can keep supporting that level of growth. This is where capacity matters and why FastScan is something to pay attention to. Management noted that this AI-enabled faster scanning technology has increased schedule throughput by ~40% while improving image clarity. This basically means LMRI can potentially be ~1.6x its entire throughput efficiency once it is 100% rolled out (rollout was ~50% complete by the end of 2025). The current plan is for adoption to reach about two-thirds by the end of 2026. Other effective measures to "expand" capacity include a virtual cockpit for remote MRI scanning, which should help with staffing flexibility and even center hours. My view is that if LMRI can push more advanced scans on a per-center basis, the growth algorithm becomes much clearer.

De novo Centers

For context, "de novo" simply means centers that LMRI opens itself, rather than buying an existing site. My view here is that if LMRI can open new centers organically, it serves as a very clean data point to show that LMRI can continue to compound growth over time (i.e., this is a better "growth" algorithm than just rolling up existing sites). Management said they opened nine de novo centers in 2025, which was a record by the way, and they also said that the typical de novo reaches breakeven in about one year. This is a very quick return on investment timeline, and management said the 2024 and 2025 cohorts are tracking in line with that expectation. With this data, we can reasonably plot out when we would see an earnings growth acceleration. The six facilities opened in 1H25 should start to contribute to 2026 earnings, and those opened this year (2026) should start contributing in 2027. This also means that the current P&L margin is not reflecting the true underlying margin (i.e., the adj. EBITDA margin should be higher).

Balance Sheet and Capital Allocation

The LMRI balance sheet is also improving, which is nice. They ended Q4 2025 with $58.8 million of cash (it was $26.1 million at the end of 2024) and have also brought leverage down from 5.5x to 3.5x. LMRI's balance sheet strength matters here because it gives LMRI more room to fund de novo projects, upgrade equipment, and also add new service lines more aggressively. The capital allocation framework also looks better now. Management laid out three priorities: (1) fund de novo growth and equipment upgrades, (2) go for selective tuck-in M&A, and (3) reduce leverage to less than 3x over time. I thought the focus on 1 and 3 being mentioned explicitly is reassuring, especially with management saying 2026 is not dependent on acquisitions. Overall, I think management is sending a good signal to the market here, and if all progresses well, LMRI will look less like a levered growth story and more like a company with a clearer path to funding its own growth.

Valuation

I believe the path to LMRI achieving the long-term revenue growth guide of 6% (at the midpoint) has become clearer. With the updated FY25 numbers, my current math is as follows:

  1. FY25 $1.023 billion, growing at 6% CAGR, would mean a revenue of $1.21 billion in FY28.
  2. FY25 adj. EBITDA margin is already at 22.3%, in the expected long-term target range. There's a good chance we see margin go above the 23% high-end given the growth and fixed cost leverage at this pace. Assuming 24% (4Q25 is already 23.8%), adj. EBITDA for FY28 should be $290.4 million.
  3. The market has pushed LMRI's valuation down to 8x NTM EBITDA (also pushed RadNet (RDNT) valuation down to 13x). Basically, the gap between the two is about the same (was 6x back then). But say that valuation stays at 8x; this implies an enterprise value of $2.3 billion, or a market cap of $1 billion. That is a share price target of ~$10.4. The upside remains attractive.

Risk

The bull case is dependent on more de novo centers ramping up on time, which may not happen if staffing, equipment installation, or local demand takes longer than expected. I am also expecting FastScan to lift throughput efficiency, which may not be as material as I thought if adoption is slower than expected. Ultimately, if demand is really strong, LMRI would need to ramp up de novos, and that could weigh on near-term margins if there is a surge in openings.

Conclusion

I reiterate my Buy rating on LMRI. My view is that Q4 2025 made the medium-term earnings setup clearer. Advanced imaging is doing well, recent de novos should start contributing, and lower leverage gives management more room to keep funding growth without stretching the balance sheet again. There will likely be some noise around timing and ramp costs. But overall, I think the core story improved this quarter, and the current setup is still attractive.

data.title: Scorpio Tankers - Looking Closer At 2026E Oil Transportation
data.description:
Over the past few months, I have increased my analysis and coverage of the shipping sector. I've looked at TORM, and I've looked at Scandinavian shipping companies. Now the time has come to take a look at one of the more prominent and perhaps more generally well-known shipping companies, Scorpio Tankers (STNG). Unlike the others I've covered (at least some of them), this company comes with an NYSE ticker despite its Monaco base.

Much like its peers, the company operates a relatively modern fleet of tankers, and the focus, as with Frontline, is the transportation of various forms of fuel, oils, and other refined products. The company is relatively young, with a founding back in '09. What differs Scorpio from most of the companies I've looked at is that, unlike the companies covered previously, Scorpio is well along in the process of having eco-vessels. By fitting all of their vessels with significant emission scrubbing, the company has reached a point where the vast majority of its fleet can be considered eco-friendly - about 85% or so.

When comparing Scorpio to its peers, it's in no way wrong to say that the company has one of the "youngest" transportation fleets on the entire market, which obviously comes with both marketing and financial advantages. Why financial?

Aside from the obvious advantage of modern vessels using less fuel and maintenance, the adoption of green technology makes it possible for the companies in question to avoid rising carbon taxes - something the EU is particularly heavy on levying through the EU ETS—as well as avoid penalties for not complying with the sulphur tax, as some older vessels seem to want to do. The comparative asset value of lower vessels in the light of rising regulation will likely be declining more and more, and therefore, Scorpio is at heart an attractive company to look at.

Let's look at what else we have here.

Scorpio Tankers - Attractive and Perhaps Less Cyclical Than Most of Its Peers

One thing when I started looking at Scorpio was that I noted the company's share price history implies much less volatility than other shipping companies of the same type. It piqued my interest, so I set out to find out why this was the case.

As with all analyses, I started by looking at the filings and financials. I quickly found some signs and clarity as to why this was the case. Since about 4 years ago, the company has used whatever cash it has gotten to pay down significant debt, well over $2B, which has fundamentally transformed the typical shippers' balance sheet. We now have a balance sheet that is closing on net cash - and for a shipper, which typically operates a high-leverage, high-volatility, high-CapEx/OpEx sheet, this is materially different and improved. This is one.

But it enables me, as a valuation analyst and fundamentally oriented investor, to speak to you with clarity about the advantages of operating exactly this sort of balance sheet.

Let's say that Scorpio Tankers was to operate with net cash or very low debt (which the company, in some ways, already, at the time of writing, is doing). Obviously, debt reduction means less interest costs. This is one of the line items that typically eats up a lot of the cash flow. But more importantly, this impact means that the company's break-even has gone lower than many of its peers. I have written about Frontline, which had an operating breakeven in the low $20,000s for one ship. This was considered excellent. But would you be surprised if I told you that at some periods, STNG's break-even is as low as $12,500, or, according to management, as low as $11,000?

The modern fleet helps with this, for sure. But it's the dual power of asking investors (who are usually used to very high yields from shipping companies) to wait for returns while they address the debt situation. The company's dividend is one of the lower ones in the industry. At the time of writing, it stands at sub-risk-free rates, at a level of 2.38%.

That the share price has not been volatile does, of course, not mean that earnings history has not been volatile. It has. Nothing can take away from the volatility of the sector, and nothing has.

The trends in OI carry over, for the most part, into most other GAAP-related KPIs that I tend to use for analyzing. But perhaps more important is the decline in interest expense that the company has seen since 2021-2022, which is now at levels not seen in over 10 years.

Due to Scorpio leasing its vessels only to some of the highest-quality oil majors, the company has very low counterparty risk. It calls it a "blue-chip" customer base, and given what the customer base consists of, I would go ahead and agree firmly with this.

The company, technically speaking, has a higher fleet average age than Frontline, though not by much. And the core of the thesis is this: due to its reduction in debt, it has created a position where, in the case of relatively contextual small increases in pricing for its vessels, it can generate significant upside. As it stands, every increase of $10,000 per day generates almost $350M in annualized cash flow.

As with Frontline, the arguments for the company consist of and include closures of older refineries in previous legacy areas, which are reshaping the way that crude flows across the seas. It increases shipping activity across the globe, and given the current geopolitical situation, I don't see a whole lot of reason why this would change. It's also fair to say, using the company's own material as a source, that the company is leading in modern product tankers.

I believe history and pricing for newbuilding have shown that Scorpio Tankers has positioned itself very attractively for the coming market trends, with those coming market trends being high prices for shipping, high prices for newbuilds, and high prices for the services associated with them, as older vessels will have to be scrapped or used differently.

Bears on shipping companies can argue ESG and electrification as they wish, but there is no coming away from the fact that electrification is, in fact, still, and likely to remain, tied to the legacy market. Demand for seaborne product transport is likely to increase, or even in the very bearish scenario, stay stable. This is not only supported by refinery closures across APAC, Europe, and NA, but also by geopolitics. The most obvious current example is Iran - though for Scorpio, new exports from Venezuela are also a part of it.

Scorpio Tankers is therefore not only well-capitalized, but it is also what I would consider to be extremely well-capitalized and prepared. With cash/equivalents of close to a billion dollars in addition to revolvers, the company has about $1.7B of credit and cash on hand to cover any newbuilding commitments until beyond 2028, none of which are likely to even come close to straining this. The company is, in fact, currently net cash on the order of over $300M.

This means that among its peers, Scorpio Tankers is probably the company that is best prepared to take advantage of the operating leverage its situation and the market situation provide. It is categorically and logically better positioned than any peer with debt unless that peer has a similar fleet or a similar business model (which most do not).

4Q25, the latest report, already confirms some of this. Earnings are up, and the company also increased its dividend (although again, I consider the dividend probably the company's weak point on a comparative basis). We'll have to see how long sanctions and other issues close the market not only for Russian crude but for similar products as well. The fact that the "Russian shadow fleet" is a target for detention and action was confirmed less than 2 weeks ago, when Sweden seized the captain of a Russian vessel under suspicion of breaking the law - the ship is not, and is unlikely to be, released anytime soon.

I consider this likely to continue, with Great Britain having already confirmed the same.

In short, I believe Scorpio Tankers to be in a good position in terms of macro. I believe this position is backed by one of the, if not the, strongest balance sheets in the sector. This is an attractive position to be in. What's more, I give management an "A" rating in capital allocation and management because, from the time the company became higher-priced, management has not been buying back any significant amount of shares.

What you see above is a trend I consider to be one of the expert capital allocators. There are times when buybacks make sense - such as when you're buying stakes in company assets at below their very obvious market prices. But too often as of late, I have looked at companies that I consider executing share buybacks for the "wrong reasons".

This, dear readers, is not one of those companies.

Let me move on to valuation

Valuation for Scorpio Tankers

So, let me take a moment to step away from the positivity I have just been describing to clearly state that Scorpio is tied to the spot market for commodities. Therefore, a crash in spot market TCE rates or any of the correlated markets will and should impact this company's valuation and income. It has, and the company's fundamental improvements won't change that.

The typical valuation model for a shipping company takes its NAV. This is one of the approaches I also use with my peers. I will use the same logic I use with Frontline, which argues that in a bear market, asset value multiples can and should drop to somewhere between 0.55x and 0.8x or so. In a bullish market, as we are now, the NAV multiple will, consequently, rise to above 1x, and there will likely be a premium to the company. To get an upside, you can therefore either buy the company in a bear market at lows or in a bullish market below premium - the former is safer but unlikely to be possible for the near-term future. Unlike Frontline, the company's share price has not been close to NAV yet, and the current price/NAV per share considered by most analysts is still below 0.85x. However, it should be said that record lows can sometimes go as low as 0.4x here.

I would therefore, as a starting point, categorize 0.83x as a price where it's "high", but not necessarily representative of the current geopolitical trends. Secondly, I will use an EBITDA multiple. EBITDA is a good approach because the company's assets, the vessels, are subject to heavy D&A on the books, which makes P/E ratios a non-starter for shipping companies. We want to use EBITDA to see what cash is flowing and where. Shipping companies tend to be between 4x and 9.5x EBITDA, with Scorpio averaging between 5x and 8x (at extremes, it's been at 22x, but I shall ignore this as an outlier). I will use an NTM estimate instead of an LTM due to the current geopolitical situation. This puts the current EBITDA multiple at 6.88x, relative to a 10-year mean of 8.5x, but this includes the surge, so make it 7.8x. This implies to me, both from a NAV multiple and from an EBITDA multiple, that there might be room for the company to move up.

This is especially true given the current state of geopolitics.

Analysts tend to agree with my target ranges. I would argue a low of 6x EBITDA and a high of 8x, which, based on conservative assumptions, would put a floor of $66 and a high of about $87/share. The analyst averages go from $70/share to $90/share as a high, with a current average PT of $81/share. Out of 8 analysts, more than 75% of them are considering the company a "BUY".

I will state that $75/share may be undervaluing this company for a number of reasons. First off, NAV. Most analysts put the NAV at the very least at $85/share. I agree with that assessment. In this environment, buying a ship (which is what you are doing if you buy shares) at a 15-20% discount is, by definition relative to the market, a good valuation deal.

And I am all about good valuation deals.

Furthermore, this share price underestimates the impact of surges in TCE. While we don't know how long the surges in TCE rates will last, it's a material and objective fact that the companies in the sector will see cash flow during 2026 from these surges. Scorpio is set to earn a lot more. This needs to be considered, and I do not see that being the case here.

Third, the net debt (or lack of it). I don't see that the company is being premiumized for its net cash, which it, in my view, should be.

As a result of this, I will give the company a PT of $76.5/share, which fulfills my requirement of 15% annualized or above to a fair value, inclusive of the dividend. Note that the company's fair value is different from the price target and instead represents the price at which I would consider the company fully valued (and potentially a rotation target).

At $76.5, the upside of 15% or above per year is still slim, but it is there. I may be considered more conservative than some (or most), but this is once again because of my "bar" in terms of returns. The fact that the company's yield is still low impacts this assumption quite heavily.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a $76.5/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is now doing, based on an upside to the company's fair value (not PT). I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "BUY" rating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a price target of $76.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: Meta: A Deeper Look At Its Capex Burdens
data.description:
Performance assessment

I was very wrong on my bullish view on Meta (META):

Elevator pitch

I am doing a 180 degree turn on my stance on Meta:

  1. Child safety regulations and addiction lawsuits can threaten user engagement and growth metrics
  2. Funding of own power generation infrastructure can rack up capex costs
  3. Market flows are shifting away from the Mag7 and social media companies
  4. META stock is valued at an absolute and relative discount
  5. The charts show a bearish rollover

Child safety regulations and addiction lawsuits can threaten user engagement and growth metrics

The world is increasingly moving to ban social media for young adults. In my recent Reddit (RDDT) article, I had shared the following:

Social media is getting more addictive. And there is an ever-growing body of research warning about the detrimental impacts of that addiction on mental health and productivity. Last year, some governments, such as Denmark, started to plan for bans on social media access for youth. My country of Australia has led the way in becoming the first nation to ban social media for teens in late Dec'25. And other countries such as New Zealand, Malaysia, the UK and more are expected to follow as curbing social media access to youth is becoming a worldwide trend.

- Hunting Alphas' article on Reddit

That said, I will mention that the broader trend of global social media bans has only gained further traction as the Czech Republic and Indonesia have also joined the wagon to outright ban social media for people aged under 15 and 16, respectively.

In addition to the social media ban headwinds, Meta is also facing a barrage of lawsuits. For example, it was fined $365M for violating child safety laws and $4.2M for inducing social media addiction:

One ruling was that the company's apps failed to protect children from sexual exploitation, for which it was fined $375M, and the other held it liable for inducing social media addiction in a young woman and was penalized about $4.2M.

However, the latter ruling by the LA jury has stirred a serious buzz, with many calling it a “Big Tobacco” moment, as the case is being labeled a bellwether for pending and upcoming lawsuits to hold social media platforms accountable for the addictive design of their products.

- Seeking Alpha News

Now, these penalty amounts are peanuts to Meta, making up only 0.63% of the company's FY25 PAT in total. However, the bigger risk impact is that the success of these lawsuits makes Meta vulnerable to a lot more similar lawsuits in future. And even more importantly, I expect it to accelerate the trends of anti-social media controls, particularly on young adults. And I posit that this will hurt social media companies like Meta via the following mechanism:

Many people get habituated to using Facebook and Instagram, and for that matter, other social media platforms, during their school and college days, when they have more friends and free time. So curbing access to social media during these young ages reduces the window of opportunity for people to become loyal, high-frequency social media users or addicts.

At the end of the day, if my thesis here is right, we should expect DAPs to see a slower rate of growth, or even a decline:

Funding of own power generation infrastructure can rack up capex costs

US policy direction is moving toward requiring major tech companies such as Meta that run large data centers to pay for their own electricity going forward for new data centers:

Tonight, I'm pleased to announce that I have negotiated the new ratepayer protection pledge. You know what that is? We're telling the major tech companies that they have the obligation to provide for their own power needs... We have an old grid. It could never handle the kind of numbers, the amount of electricity that's needed. So I'm telling them, they can build their own plant. They're going to produce their own electricity.

- US President Donald Trump

Recently, Meta went into an agreement with Entergy (ETR), wherein Meta will be footing the bill for:

  • Seven new natural gas-fueled combined-cycle power plants totaling more than 5,200 megawatts, with capability for future carbon capture and hydrogen co-firing
  • Approximately 240 miles of new 500 kV transmission lines connecting South Louisiana to North Louisiana and Arkansas
  • Battery energy storage across three locations
  • Nuclear power uprates
  • Commitment from Meta to help fund up to 2,500 megawatts of new renewable resources
  • Memorandum of understanding to explore the future development and use of nuclear power

- Entergy Press Release, HA's bolded highlights

I expect this to impact Meta's $10B 1GW Indiana data center as well.

So the critical question is what would the financial impact of all this be? Here's how I'm working through it:

First, I computed the cost rates for power and transmission line infrastructure using known information from a similar Meta - Entergy deal last year:

Then, based on this power infrastructure cost rate of 1.41 USD/W and transmission line cost rate of 2,400 USD/V, I computed the total infrastructure cost of the latest Entergy deal to be $8.6B:

But I still compute total power and transmission cost rates based on data center values and data center capacities for this project. This gives me a crude average for power and transmission infrastructure for data centers:

This lets me estimate the power and transmission infrastructure cost of the Indiana data center to be $2.8B:

So adding up the Entergy deal's infrastructure cost and the expected costs of the Indiana data center up, we get a total incremental cost of $11.3B:

Ultimately, from a capex impact perspective, this represents a 9.1% total cost hike, counting only these major data center projects:

Now, the chart above presents the data as if all of this infrastructure cost will be borne in FY26. Of course, some of it may spill over to FY27 too. But the broader point is that due to this new policy, pledges, capex costs can be hiked by almost 10%. And this is a clear headwind for Meta because these cost increases are not offset by higher revenues.

Market flows are shifting away from the Mag7 and social media companies

META is a core stock in the Magnificent 7 group, as can be seen in the Roundhill's Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS):

The Magnificent 7 stocks make up a large chunk of the overall S&P500 market capitalization share:

And this has contributed to overall S&P500 (SPY) outperformance vs the equal-weighted S&P500 (RSP):

But so far this YTD, the Mag7 stocks are correcting hard. And Meta has performed the 2nd-worst YTD after Microsoft (MSFT):

I believe this is due to greater headwinds hitting social media companies, something I discussed in more depth in my last piece on Reddit. Indeed, the industry rotation graphs support this narrative, as the Social Media industry factor has been the worst performing:

Overall, this is part of a broader theme wherein money flows are leaving technology and AI stocks in favor of heavy assets plays and consumer staples. Hence, from this market sentiment analysis, the verdict for META stock is not very encouraging.

META stock is valued at an absolute and relative discount

META is trading at a 1-yr FWD P/E of 17.4x, which is a moderate 33% discount to its longer-term median multiple of 25.9x.

Relative to social media company peers, META's 1-yr FWD P/E is 10% higher than the comps' median of 15.8x:

But this does not mean overvaluation. In fact, it suggests the opposite because Meta has typically commanded a much higher premium vs its peers of 22.8%:

So the lower 10% premium now indicates relative undervaluation.

The charts show a bearish rollover

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

META Technical Analysis

META stock is displaying strong bearish momentum due to 2 bearish engulfs after a rejection from monthly resistance. And there is still some downside left until the next major monthly support level is hit.

Takeaway & Positioning

As a recap, this shows the breakup of my view on X stock based on different drivers, as well as an overall assessment:

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bearish due to headwinds from social media bans, more potential addiction lawsuits, and an almost 10% capex cost hike
  • Sentiment Analysis: Bearish due to rotation out of Mag7 and especially social media stocks
  • Valuation: Bullish due to discounted valuations
  • Technical Analysis: Bearish due to strong seller momentum
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bearish, 'Sell.'

• The fund posted returns of 1.28% (Institutional shares) and 1.22% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2025.

• The fund's outperformance of its benchmark was led by agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), investment grade and high yield credit, duration (interest rate sensitivity) and yield curve positioning, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), and asset-backed securities (ABS).

• The fund's long duration position was increased, which reflected an economic backdrop that was supportive of potential Federal Reserve (Fed) monetary policy easing and a path of least resistance toward lower yields. Spread assets tightened and the fund selectively trimmed exposure in areas of richness.

Contributors

Overweight exposures to both agency MBS and investment grade credit were the largest contributors to performance. The low volatility caused by a decrease in Fed policy uncertainty supported MBS valuation significantly, with favorable supply and demand a tailwind for corporate credit, while years of above-trend growth have strengthened corporates' financial positions. As such, demand from all-in yield buyers has been robust, which has allowed spreads to compress. Similar themes apply to ABS and CMBS, which performed positively overall.

Detractors

An underweight exposure to U.S. Treasuries marginally detracted, as the sector did well during the quarter.
