data.title: Micron: Buy This Selloff Driven By TurboQuant And Sora
data.description:
Introduction

In my previous article on Micron Technology (MU), I laid out the case for why the memory industry has structurally changed. The combination of slowing DRAM scaling, rising capital intensity, and strong AI-driven demand led me to believe that the traditional boom-bust cycle in memory is unlikely to repeat, making Micron a compelling investment.

Since then, Micron has reported a blowout quarter, but the stock has pulled back anyway. In this update, I want to break down what drove the decline, and more importantly, why I believe the current concerns make this an attractive buying opportunity.

Earnings

On March 18, Micron delivered an FQ2 topline beat of 22.3% and a bottom line beat of 39.7%. Even more impressive, Micron delivered a top-line guidance beat of 44% and a bottom-line guidance beat of 77.8%.

The sentiment afterward was euphoric! For a company with a market cap nearing half a trillion to deliver this kind of beat screamed of another NVIDIA (NVDA) moment. However, the immediate reaction was a steep decline, which left many investors scratching their heads.

I believe the reason for the reaction was the increased CAPEX guide to $25B from $20B. Historically speaking, the time to buy a memory stock has been after a lot of capacity has left the industry and there's a catalyst that will trigger an increase in demand. Conversely, the ideal time to sell would be when there are lots of new announcements for new plants, not when the earnings turn down. The reason for this is because expansion plans typically meant that future earnings would go down, and the stock market is forward-looking. Seasoned memory cycle investors follow this playbook religiously; it doesn't matter if "this time is different".

However, there is good reason to believe this time is actually different, and seasoned memory cycle investors may be the ones that look like fools this time around, exiting too early. I won't go into depth into the structural reasons that memory is no longer cyclical since I already covered them in my first Micron article here, but what I would like to add is the confirmation of the industry's first 5-year SCA confirmed by Micron in the latest earnings call. The details of the SCA are not publicly released, but Micron's CEO Sanjay Mehrotra stated:

What I want to highlight is that these SCAs are multiyear and they have specific commitments in them. These are robust agreements, and these are meant to give us the visibility and stability toward out business model."

I believe that Sanjay's words are confirmation that the increased CAPEX guide is backed by long-term visibility, and the 5-year SCA enforces my view. If I am correct on one of the selloff drivers being the increased CAPEX guide, I believe this is an attractive buying opportunity.

TurboQuant

On March 24, Google Research introduced TurboQuant. They claimed their new compression algorithm reduces LLM key-value cache memory by at least 6 times while delivering 8 times more speed and 0 accuracy loss.

This new algorithm made headlines, added downside pressure across memory stocks, and raised some eyebrows on the future of memory demand. However, after further digging, a couple of key facts were revealed. Firstly, it was discovered that the TurboQuant paper was first submitted on April 28, 2025, so it obviously did not prevent the memory supercycle from continuing, and it makes little sense for memory stocks to sell off on something nearly a year old. Second, the TurboQuant paper contains serious issues in how it describes RaBitQ, including incorrect technical claims, misleading theory, and misleading experiment comparisons. These concerns were flagged by the RaBitQ authors before the submission of TurboQuant, and they were acknowledged but not fixed. With that being said, the huge promotion and fear created by TurboQuant seem to be very overblown and not a genuine concern. In my opinion, the TurboQuant fears should be bought.

Sora

On March 24, OpenAI announced they are shutting down Sora. This was the result of high compute costs, minimal monetization, and a strategic shift toward more focused initiatives. While this was not talked about much in regard to the memory stock sell-off, I believe this was a major driver since Sora was a prime example of highly memory-intensive AI workloads. Aside from the actual demand destruction caused by the shutdown, Sora's shutdown also raises concerns that these video generation workloads may be less economically viable than previously expected, given the massive infrastructure and memory requirements they impose. In that context, memory stocks selling off makes sense since one of the leading AI players is stepping away, leaving investors to question how sustainable the current memory demand really is.

However, I think this reaction is not justified since this likely misrepresents the true meaning behind Sora's shutdown. The issue is not a lack of demand for memory-intensive AI workloads, but rather the current cost of delivering them at scale. When costs are too high, companies are forced to optimize and improve efficiency. This does not eliminate demand; it just delays and reshapes it. If anything, the fact that Sora was shut down shows how much demand there truly is and how resource-heavy those workloads truly are. Sora's shutdown will leave video generation demand building beneath the surface, waiting for the next stage of optimization and deployment to unlock it once again. As efficiency improves and infrastructure scales, I'm confident more platforms similar to Sora will return in a more economically viable and sustainable form.

Risks

While I view Micron's increased CAPEX and 5-year SCA as a sign of long-term visibility, the market is clearly still seeing it through the traditional boom-and-bust cycle lens. If all three major players, Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix, ramp up capacity too aggressively, it could recreate the supply dynamics seen in previous cycles. However, I believe this risk is insignificant due to the structural trends in the memory industry that I covered in my first Micron article here.

TurboQuant, while flawed, also highlights a potential future risk. As the industry tries to reduce memory requirements through efficiency gains and compression, the rapidly increasing demand could be challenged. As of now, there is nothing to indicate these efficiency gains are disrupting demand, but I will be sure to monitor any future developments and update readers when a notable shift occurs.

Conclusion

I believe this recent selloff is being driven more by short-term fear around CAPEX, TurboQuant, and Sora's shutdown than any real deterioration in Micron's long-term demand outlook. While these developments have understandably raised confusion and concerns around efficiency gains reducing memory demand and the viability of video generation workloads, I view them as noise within a much greater structural shift in the memory industry. When viewed alongside multi-year SCA agreements highlighting demand visibility, I see this pullback as a buying opportunity rather than a warning sign.

Thus, I would like to reiterate my rating of MU as a "Strong Buy."
